Exam questions ask which IoT scenarios improve life (smart traffic lights reducing emissions) and which raise privacy concerns (always-on microphones).

questions.prompt: Subset of AI that learns from data to make predictions without being explicitly programmed.
questions.explanation: Machine learning (Arthur Samuel, IBM 1959 — checkers-playing program) gives computers ability to learn from data. Modern boom: AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al. 2012 — won ImageNet with deep CNNs), ResNet (He et al. 2015), Transformers (Vaswani et al. 'Attention Is All You Need' 2017 — underpins GPT, BERT), Generative AI (ChatGPT Nov 2022). AP CSP Big Idea 5.2 and 5.5 cover ML's beneficial (medical diagnosis, accessibility) and harmful (bias, deepfakes, job displacement) effects. Exam questions present an ML scenario and ask students to identify benefits (scale, pattern detection) and risks (bias, opacity, manipulation).

questions.prompt: False or misleading information spread online with intent to deceive.
questions.explanation: Misinformation (unintentional false info) and disinformation (intentional) spread faster than truth on social media (Vosoughi et al. Science 2018 — falsehoods spread 6× faster than truth on Twitter). Examples: 2016 election (Russian IRA, McFaul testimony), COVID-19 (5G conspiracy, ivermectin), 2021 Capitol attack mobilized by online claims. AP CSP Big Idea 5.5 covers misinformation as a harmful effect of computing. Defenses: fact-checkers (Snopes 1994, PolitiFact 2007), platform moderation, media literacy. Exam questions ask why social media accelerates misinformation (algorithmic amplification of engagement, low cost of distribution, echo chambers).

questions.prompt: Unequal accuracy of AI systems across demographic groups — like facial recognition working worse on darker skin.
questions.explanation: Algorithmic bias is unfair outcome that comes from training data, model design, or deployment context. Joy Buolamwini's MIT thesis 'Gender Shades' (2018) found IBM, Microsoft, and Megvii face APIs were 35% less accurate on dark-skinned women. ProPublica's 2016 COMPAS audit found it twice as likely to flag Black defendants as future criminals. Amazon's 2018 recruiting AI penalized 'women's' resumes. AP CSP Big Idea 5.5 makes bias a central exam topic — expect 2-3 MC questions. Mitigation: diverse training data, fairness audits (AIF360 IBM, Fairlearn Microsoft), disparate-impact testing.

questions.prompt: Right of individuals to know how their personal data is collected, stored, and used.
questions.explanation: Data privacy gives individuals control over their personal information. Samuel Warren and Louis Brandeis' 1890 Harvard Law Review article 'The Right to Privacy' established the modern concept. GDPR (EU 2018) introduced 'right to be forgotten,' data portability, breach notification within 72 hours; CCPA (California 2020) gave US consumers similar rights. AP CSP Big Idea 5.6 covers data privacy. The 2018 Cambridge Analytica scandal (87M Facebook profiles harvested without consent) accelerated regulation. Exam questions present a data practice and ask whether it respects privacy (asking opt-in consent) or violates it (default-on tracking, dark patterns).

questions.prompt: Right to remove embarrassing or harmful content about yourself from search engine results.
questions.explanation: Right to be forgotten (RTBF) was established by EU Court of Justice in Google Spain v AEPD (May 2014) and codified in GDPR Article 17 (2018). Users may request search engines de-list outdated or irrelevant results about them; Google has processed ~5M URL removal requests. Tension: balancing privacy against freedom of expression, public interest, and historical record. US largely rejects RTBF on First Amendment grounds. AP CSP Big Idea 5.6 covers this evolving right. Exam questions present a takedown scenario (job applicant with old news article) and ask which privacy framework supports the request.

questions.prompt: Practice of users sealing themselves into communities where they only hear views they already hold.
questions.explanation: Echo chambers result from algorithmic personalization (Facebook News Feed 2006, YouTube recommender 2010s) showing users content similar to what they engage with. Eli Pariser's 2011 'The Filter Bubble' coined the related term. Cass Sunstein's 2001 'Republic.com' warned of group polarization. Research is mixed: Bakshy et al. (Science 2015) found Facebook's algorithm contributes less to echo chambers than self-selected friendships. AP CSP Big Idea 5.5 covers echo chambers as a harm of recommender systems. Exam questions distinguish algorithmic causes (personalization, engagement optimization) from human ones (selective exposure) and ask which interventions could reduce polarization.

questions.prompt: Set of behaviors and responsibilities expected of users in digital spaces.
questions.explanation: Digital citizenship covers respectful, responsible, and safe behavior online. ISTE Standards (International Society for Technology in Education, 2007 revised 2016) define student standards. Mike Ribble's 'Nine Elements of Digital Citizenship' framework (2007) is widely taught. AP CSP Big Idea 5.1 touches on digital citizenship — students should consider impact on others. Exam questions present a scenario (cyberbullying response, posting someone's photo without consent) and ask which choice exhibits good digital citizenship. Citizenship includes critical thinking about sources, respect for IP, and care with personal data.

questions.prompt: European Union law that gives users rights over their personal data — passed in 2018.
questions.explanation: GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation, EU 2018) gives users right to know, access, correct, delete, and port their personal data. Companies must obtain explicit consent, notify breaches within 72 hours, and appoint Data Protection Officers. Fines: up to 4% of global revenue or €20M (Meta fined €1.2B in May 2023). GDPR extraterritoriality: applies to any company processing EU residents' data. AP CSP Big Idea 5.6 covers GDPR as a major privacy framework. The California CCPA (2020) and Virginia VCDPA (2023) mirror many GDPR concepts. Exam questions identify which rights GDPR grants (right to erasure, portability).

questions.prompt: Practice of breaching computer systems for malicious purposes — illegal under federal law.
questions.explanation: Hacking (malicious sense) is unauthorized system access. The US Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA, 1986) is the foundational anti-hacking law. Famous cases: Kevin Mitnick (1995 arrest after FBI pursuit), Aaron Swartz (2011 JSTOR case, suicide 2013 sparked CFAA reform debate), the 2014 Sony Pictures hack (attributed to North Korea), 2020 SolarWinds (Russia). 'White hat' hackers do authorized testing; the bug bounty industry (HackerOne 2012) pays them. AP CSP Big Idea 5.6 covers hacking as a major threat. Exam questions distinguish ethical hacking (with permission) from criminal hacking.

questions.prompt: Crime of using someone else's personal information to commit fraud — like opening accounts in their name.
questions.explanation: Identity theft uses stolen PII (name, SSN, DOB) to open fraudulent accounts, file false tax returns, or commit medical fraud. The 2017 Equifax breach exposed 147M Americans' SSNs and is a feedstock for ongoing fraud. FTC's Consumer Sentinel reported ~1.4M identity theft cases in 2023. AP CSP Big Idea 5.6 covers identity theft as a consequence of poor data security. Defenses: credit freezes (free since 2018), unique passwords + password manager, MFA, monitoring services. The Fair Credit Reporting Act (1970) gives victims rights to dispute fraudulent records. Exam questions ask what data points enable identity theft (SSN, DOB, financial account numbers).

questions.prompt: Authentication standard that lets you sign in to many apps using one account — like Google Sign-In.
questions.explanation: SSO (single sign-on) lets one identity provider authenticate users across many apps. OAuth 2.0 (Eran Hammer, IETF RFC 6749, 2012) and OpenID Connect (OIDC, 2014) are the modern standards. SAML (2001) is the enterprise predecessor. Examples: 'Sign in with Google,' 'Sign in with Apple' (privacy-focused 2019), Microsoft Azure AD, Okta (founded 2009). AP CSP Big Idea 5.6 covers SSO as both convenience (one strong password + MFA) and risk (one compromise = many accounts). Exam questions ask the trade-offs: SSO simplifies UX and centralizes security, but creates a single point of failure. MFA is critical with SSO.

questions.prompt: Iterative four-step practice that produces working software through repeated refinement.
questions.explanation: The program design process — investigate, design, prototype, test, refine — is at the heart of AP CSP Big Idea 1. Margaret Hamilton (Apollo flight software lead, 1960s) and Grace Hopper (FLOW-MATIC compiler 1955) helped institutionalize iteration. The Create Performance Task scoring rubric explicitly requires students to document at least one iteration of program development. Exam MC questions ask 'what is the next step?' in a design scenario — almost always: test what you just built.

questions.prompt: Reusable named block of code with parameters — required in every Create Performance Task.
questions.explanation: Procedures are essential — the Create PT rubric awards a point specifically for 'student-developed procedure with a parameter that affects functionality.' AP CSP reference sheet uses PROCEDURE name(parameters) { body }. Pioneer Maurice Wilkes (EDSAC, Cambridge 1949) wrote one of the first papers on subroutines and the call stack. Without a working parameterized procedure, students cannot pass the Create PT. The procedure's parameter must actually change behavior — a dummy unused parameter loses the point.

questions.prompt: Strategy of breaking a big problem into smaller, manageable subproblems.
questions.explanation: Decomposition is one of Jeannette Wing's four pillars of computational thinking (CACM 2006). Breaking 'build a Snake game' into smaller procedures (move snake, detect collision, draw board, handle keyboard) makes the problem tractable. Niklaus Wirth's 'Algorithms + Data Structures = Programs' (1976) made decomposition a core CS discipline. The Create PT scoring rewards decomposition that produces meaningful procedures — students who write one 200-line procedure lose points.

questions.prompt: Hiding implementation behind a clean interface — the second of Jeannette Wing's CT pillars.
questions.explanation: Abstraction is the most-tested concept on AP CSP — Big Idea 3 covers procedural abstraction (procedures hide implementation) and data abstraction (lists hide storage). Edsger Dijkstra ('On the Role of Scientific Thought,' 1974) and Barbara Liskov (CLU language, Turing Award 2008) formalized it. The Create PT rubric awards a point for procedural abstraction that 'manages complexity.' Roughly 1 in 5 MC exam questions test abstraction concepts.

questions.prompt: Practice where two programmers work together at one workstation, sharing knowledge.
questions.explanation: Pair programming (formalized by Kent Beck in Extreme Programming, 1999) has one programmer 'driving' (typing) while the other 'navigates' (reviewing, planning). Studies show modest cost increase (~15% more programmer time) but significantly fewer defects. AP CSP Big Idea 1.3 covers collaboration. Pair programming benefits include knowledge transfer (junior learning from senior), reduced bus-factor risk, and immediate code review. Exam questions ask which collaboration style is described (pair programming, code review, async via Git).

questions.prompt: Base-2 representation — the foundation of all digital data.
questions.explanation: Binary (base-2) is fundamental — every transistor in a CPU is on (1) or off (0). Gottfried Leibniz formalized binary in 1703. Claude Shannon's 1937 MIT thesis bridged Boolean algebra and digital logic. AP CSP Big Idea 2.1 tests decimal-binary conversion (5 = 101) and the relationship n bits = 2^n values. With 8 bits you represent 256 values; 16 bits = 65,536; 32 bits = 4.3 billion. Exam questions ask the minimum bits needed for N values — round up log2(N).

questions.prompt: Compression style used by JPEG and MP3 that throws away some data.
questions.explanation: Lossy compression (JPEG, MP3, MPEG) gets much higher compression ratios by discarding perceptually less-important data. JPEG (Joint Photographic Experts Group, 1992) drops high-frequency color detail; MP3 (Karlheinz Brandenburg 1989) drops sounds masked by louder ones. AP CSP Big Idea 2.1 contrasts lossy (photos, music) with lossless (source code, medical records). Exam questions force a choice: an executable file → lossless (any bit loss breaks it); a vacation photo → lossy (acceptable visual loss for 10× smaller file).

questions.prompt: Information about other information — like a photo's date, GPS coordinates, and camera model.
questions.explanation: Metadata describes other data. A photo's EXIF metadata includes timestamp, GPS coordinates, camera model, ISO, shutter speed. AP CSP Big Idea 2.2.1 stresses that metadata enables organization (search 'photos from 2023') but also leaks privacy (GPS in EXIF revealed John McAfee's hideout in 2012). Email headers, MP3 ID3 tags, Word document author info — all metadata. Exam questions ask students to identify metadata (when/where/who, not what).

questions.prompt: Encoding standard that maps numbers 0-127 to common English characters like A=65.
questions.explanation: ASCII (1963 ANSI X3.4) encodes 128 characters in 7 bits. 'A' = 65 (01000001), 'a' = 97, '0' = 48, ' ' (space) = 32. Bob Bemer ('father of ASCII') led the standardization. ASCII could not handle non-English scripts, so Unicode (1991) and UTF-8 (Ken Thompson 1992) followed — UTF-8 represents 1.1M Unicode code points using 1-4 bytes. AP CSP Big Idea 2.1.5 covers character encoding. Exam questions: 'how many bits does ASCII use?' (7); 'what value represents A?' (65).

questions.prompt: Skewed pattern in data that comes from how it was collected — can lead to biased AI predictions.
questions.explanation: Data bias arises when training data does not represent the deployment population. The 1936 Literary Digest poll predicted Landon over FDR because it sampled phone owners (wealthy, Republican). Joy Buolamwini's 'Gender Shades' (MIT 2018) showed facial recognition was 35% less accurate on dark-skinned women due to under-representation in training data. AP CSP Big Idea 5.5 covers algorithmic bias — biased data trains biased models. Exam questions describe a sampling method and ask whether the sample is representative.

questions.prompt: Step-by-step finite procedure for solving a problem — named after the 9th-century mathematician al-Khwarizmi.
questions.explanation: The word 'algorithm' comes from al-Khwarizmi (Persian mathematician, ~825 CE) whose Latin name became 'algoritmi.' Donald Knuth's 'The Art of Computer Programming' (3 vol, 1968-onwards) is the canonical reference. Alan Turing's 1936 paper 'On Computable Numbers' formalized algorithms via Turing machines. AP CSP Big Idea 3.5 covers algorithm fundamentals — built from sequencing, selection, iteration (Böhm-Jacopini theorem 1966). About 25-30% of MC exam questions trace or analyze algorithms.

questions.prompt: AP CSP reference-sheet construct that loops UNTIL a condition becomes true.
questions.explanation: REPEAT UNTIL (condition) in AP CSP reference sheet — the condition is checked at the top, so the loop body might run 0 times. Opposite of 'while' in most languages (while loops while-true; REPEAT UNTIL loops while-NOT-true). AP CSP Big Idea 3.9 covers iteration. The biggest bug source is infinite loops — forgetting to update the loop variable. Exam questions show a REPEAT UNTIL block and ask how many times it runs for given initial values.

questions.prompt: Algorithm that scales as n!, 2^n, or worse — impractical for large inputs.
questions.explanation: AP CSP Big Idea 3.17.2 names algorithms that scale exponentially (2^n, n!) as 'unreasonable.' Brute-force Traveling Salesman is O(n!) — 20 cities = 2.4 × 10^18 paths. P vs NP (Stephen Cook 1971) is the famous open question of whether every efficiently-checkable problem is also efficiently solvable. Cryptography exploits hard problems: factoring large integers is currently unreasonable, which makes RSA secure. Exam questions compare algorithm efficiency and identify which scales unreasonably.

questions.prompt: Programming construct that performs different actions depending on a Boolean expression.
questions.explanation: Selection (IF/ELSE in AP CSP reference) lets a program respond to data — different action for empty input vs valid. Boolean algebra (George Boole 1854) is the math foundation. AP CSP Big Idea 3.8 covers conditionals including nested IFs and compound Boolean expressions (AND, OR, NOT). The Create PT rubric Row 5 requires either selection or iteration in the student-developed algorithm. Exam questions test De Morgan's laws applied to nested IF conditions.

questions.prompt: Practical 'good enough' solution used when the exact algorithm is too slow — like Google Maps' route finder.
questions.explanation: Heuristics trade optimality for speed. Google Maps uses A* (Hart, Nilsson, Raphael 1968), not exact Dijkstra (1959). Simulated annealing (Kirkpatrick et al. 1983) and genetic algorithms (John Holland 1975) are heuristic frameworks. AP CSP Big Idea 3.18 covers heuristics as a response to unreasonable algorithms. Trade-off: heuristics may produce wrong or sub-optimal answers in rare cases. Exam questions ask why a heuristic is acceptable when an exact algorithm would take centuries.

questions.prompt: Set of rules letting different devices communicate — like TCP/IP and HTTP.
questions.explanation: Protocols are agreed-upon conventions: TCP/IP, HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS. They make heterogeneous networks interoperable. Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn's 1974 TCP/IP paper unified divergent networks. IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force) publishes RFCs defining Internet protocols. AP CSP Big Idea 4.1 covers protocols. Exam questions match protocol to purpose: HTTP→web, SMTP→sending mail, DNS→name resolution, TCP→reliable transport. Without protocols, the Internet wouldn't exist.

questions.prompt: Internet's phonebook — translates google.com to an IP address.
questions.explanation: DNS (Paul Mockapetris, USC ISI 1983) is hierarchical: root → TLD (.com) → authoritative. Recursive resolvers cache results. AP CSP Big Idea 4.1.3 covers DNS as critical infrastructure. DNS hijacking and cache poisoning are major threats (Kaminsky 2008 vulnerability). DNSSEC adds cryptographic signing. Exam questions trace a DNS lookup or ask why DNS is needed (we'd memorize IP addresses without it).

questions.prompt: Network design where messages split into independent chunks that route around failures.
questions.explanation: Packet switching breaks messages into independently-routed packets. Designed by Paul Baran (RAND 1962) and Donald Davies (NPL 1965) to be nuclear-survivable: lose a node, packets reroute. ARPANET (1969) implemented it. Today's Internet is end-to-end packet-switched. AP CSP Big Idea 4.1.4 covers this. Compared to circuit switching (1880s phone, dedicated path), packet switching is fault-tolerant, scales better, and uses bandwidth efficiently.

questions.prompt: Internet's resilience to component failure thanks to redundant paths and decentralization.
questions.explanation: Fault tolerance comes from redundancy — many paths between any two nodes. Paul Baran's 1962 RAND paper imagined a network that could survive nuclear attack. AP CSP Big Idea 4.1.5 stresses fault tolerance as core Internet design. Real-world: when AWS US-East-1 fails, services with multi-region failover keep working. Hierarchical Internet structure (Tier 1, 2, 3 ISPs) provides multiple peering paths. Exam questions ask why packet switching is more fault-tolerant than circuit switching.

questions.prompt: Computing model where work splits across many networked machines that cooperate.
questions.explanation: Distributed computing uses many networked machines for tasks too big for one. Folding@home (Stanford 2000) modeled COVID-19 proteins on volunteer computers at 2.5 exaflops in 2020 — faster than any single supercomputer. MapReduce (Google 2004) indexed the web. AP CSP Big Idea 4.2 covers distributed computing. Pioneer Leslie Lamport's 1978 'Time, Clocks, and the Ordering of Events' formalized distributed-system theory (Turing Award 2013).

questions.prompt: Gap between people with reliable Internet access and those without — affects education and healthcare.
questions.explanation: The digital divide separates broadband haves and have-nots. Term traces to NTIA's 1995 'Falling Through the Net' report. ~2.5B humans remain offline in 2024. Rural areas, low-income households, elderly, and developing nations face the largest gaps. The 2020 COVID school closures exposed this — students without home broadband fell behind. AP CSP Big Idea 5.3 covers the divide; policy responses include rural broadband subsidies (BEAD Act 2021 $42B), public Wi-Fi, school hotspots.

questions.prompt: Massive volunteer projects like Wikipedia and Galaxy Zoo that aggregate small contributions.
questions.explanation: Crowdsourcing (Jeff Howe, Wired 2006) collects many small contributions. Wikipedia (Jimmy Wales 2001, 7M articles), reCAPTCHA (Luis von Ahn 2007 — book digitization), Galaxy Zoo (~1M galaxies classified). AP CSP Big Idea 5.4 covers crowdsourcing's benefits (scale, diversity) and concerns (quality control, exploitation of unpaid labor). Exam questions distinguish crowdsourcing (many people, small contributions) from open source (collaborative software development).

questions.prompt: License framework that lets creators share work with explicit reuse permissions.
questions.explanation: Creative Commons (Lawrence Lessig, Hal Abelson, Eric Eldred — Stanford 2001) provides 6 standardized licenses. ~2B works are CC-licensed (Wikipedia, Flickr, MIT OpenCourseWare). The CC suite ranges from CC0 (public domain) to CC BY-NC-ND (most restrictive). AP CSP Big Idea 5.4 covers CC as a tool for digital sharing. Exam questions test which CC license allows commercial use (any without -NC), modification (any without -ND), and what requires attribution (all except CC0).

questions.prompt: Unfair AI outcomes that come from skewed training data or model design.
questions.explanation: Algorithmic bias is unfair outcome that comes from data, design, or deployment. Joy Buolamwini's 'Gender Shades' (MIT 2018, with Timnit Gebru) found commercial face APIs were 35% less accurate on dark-skinned women. ProPublica's 2016 COMPAS audit found it twice as likely to flag Black defendants as future criminals. Amazon's 2018 recruiting AI penalized 'women's' resumes. AP CSP Big Idea 5.5 makes bias central. Mitigations: diverse training data, fairness audits (IBM AIF360, Microsoft Fairlearn), regulatory oversight (EU AI Act 2024).

questions.prompt: Login security adding factors like phone codes or hardware keys beyond a password.
questions.explanation: MFA combines factors: something you know (password), have (phone/key), are (biometric). Reduces phishing-related account takeovers by 99%+ (Microsoft 2019). FIDO2/WebAuthn (FIDO Alliance 2018) uses public-key cryptography for phishing-resistant logins. YubiKey (Stina Ehrensvärd, Yubico 2008) popularized hardware tokens. AP CSP Big Idea 5.6 emphasizes MFA as the single biggest security improvement. Exam questions ask which factor combinations qualify (password+SMS=2FA; password+PIN=NOT, both are 'know').
